stories.body: The United States carried out a sixth night of strikes on Iranian territory aimed at degrading Tehran’s military capabilities, hitting coastal surveillance, air-defense, logistics and maritime assets near Bandar Abbas, Qeshm and Bushehr, while Iranian officials said the bombardment also damaged bridges, a train station, power infrastructure and even forced a children’s cancer hospital in Ahvaz to evacuate 211 patients; Iran’s Revolutionary Guard claimed retaliation against US-linked sites in Oman, Kuwait, Bahrain and Syria, Jordan reported intercepting incoming missiles, Kurdish fighters downed drones over Erbil, the Strait of Hormuz remained shut with global oil supplies imperiled, and total casualties in Iran since fighting resumed reportedly climbed to at least 38 dead and more than 400 injured.
stories.headline: US expands strikes across Iran as Tehran reports civilian infrastructure hit

stories.body: Canada is battling more than 850 active wildfires—over 130 in northwestern Ontario alone—and their smoke has repeatedly plunged air quality into “hazardous” territory from Toronto and Thunder Bay to Chicago, Detroit, New York and across more than 20 US states, forcing evacuations in First Nations communities, cancelling outdoor events from summer camps to concerts, prompting mask distribution and heat emergency extensions, and spurring weather services to warn of reduced visibility and persistent unhealthy air until winds shift early next week.
stories.headline: Canada’s wildfires push hazardous smoke into US cities again

stories.body: Japan’s parliament approved the first major amendments to the Imperial House Law since 1949, allowing distant male relatives over 15 to be adopted back into the monarchy and permitting princesses to retain royal status after marrying commoners in order to bolster the shrinking pool of eligible heirs, yet the reform leaves in place the male-only succession rule despite polls showing more than 70% of Japanese favor a female emperor, meaning Princess Aiko and other women remain barred from the throne even as the current line of succession could end if Prince Hisahito has no male children.
stories.headline: Japan lets distant male royals return to the family but keeps ban on female emperors

stories.body: Speaking at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Chinese President Xi Jinping warned that AI development must not be “a solo performance” dominated by any single country, called for rules that keep humans in control, pledged to work with partners across BRICS, Africa, Latin America and Asia so that developing nations share in AI capacity building, and criticized expansive national-security restrictions as Chinese firms race to challenge US rivals even amid US export controls on advanced chips.
stories.headline: Xi Jinping urges global cooperation on artificial intelligence governance

stories.body: Days of torrential rain left much of Central and South Texas under flood watches and warnings, with rivers like the Guadalupe and Pedernales spilling over, streets in Kerrville, Comfort and Uvalde rendered impassable, hundreds of rescues conducted—including of a child swept up overnight—and residents describing shortages of medicine and essential supplies as helicopters and boats continued to evacuate people from neighborhoods still recovering from catastrophic floods a year ago.
stories.headline: Deadly floods swamp Central Texas again

stories.body: Revelations that the ICE officer who fatally shot Colombian national Johan Sebastián Durán Guerrero in Biddeford, Maine, had a long history of mental health struggles and violent behavior have prompted mourning vigils, protests outside federal buildings, and calls from congressional Democrats for investigations into Homeland Security’s hiring and vetting, while Mexico formally requested US state attorneys general and the Justice Department investigate 17 deaths of Mexican immigrants in ICE custody or operations since President Trump’s second term, threatening civil suits against detention contractors.
stories.headline: ICE shootings spark nationwide scrutiny and demands for accountability

stories.body: In a primetime address, President Donald Trump declassified intelligence dossiers he said proved that China and “deep state” actors compromised 220 million US voter files during the 2020 cycle, reviving unsupported allegations about rigged elections even as experts noted the documents largely referenced publicly available voter data and offered no evidence that Beijing altered results; Democrats accused him of eroding faith in November’s midterms, while allies said election integrity will be central to his campaign.
stories.headline: Trump seizes on election security claims, releasing purported China interference files

stories.body: The Trump administration finalized regulations capping most foreign-student visas at four years, limiting foreign journalists to 240 days—90 for Chinese reporters—and resurrecting the “public charge” standard that can deny green cards to immigrants who use benefits like SNAP, Medicaid or housing vouchers, moves officials say will ensure self-reliance but critics argue will discourage legal immigration and penalize mixed-status families amid rising living costs.
stories.headline: White House tightens immigration rules on students, journalists and benefit users

stories.body: The European Union’s Court of Justice ruled that Spain’s Catalan amnesty law does not violate EU financial interests or terrorism directives, affirming Madrid’s authority to grant amnesty in pursuit of national reconciliation and signaling that pending cases at the Tribunal de Cuentas and Audiencia Nacional should be closed, yet leaving unresolved whether Supreme Court judge Pablo Llarena can keep malversation charges against Carles Puigdemont and Oriol Junqueras alive, an issue now before Spain’s Constitutional Court, which expects to apply the EU ruling later this year.
stories.headline: EU’s top court clears Catalan amnesty law

stories.body: Catalan president Salvador Illa called for the “diligent, comprehensive and loophole-free” enforcement of the newly endorsed amnesty, insisting there are no remaining democratic or European obstacles after the EU court’s ruling, while Spain’s opposition Popular Party maintained pressure by highlighting parallel corruption probes, including Begoña Gómez’s case, to argue that Socialist allies are twisting the justice system for political gain.
stories.headline: Government and Catalan leaders press courts to apply the amnesty immediately as PP keeps up attacks

stories.body: Madrid’s Provincial Court upheld Judge Juan Carlos Peinado’s decision to send Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez’s wife, Begoña Gómez, to a jury trial but limited the case to alleged influence peddling over her Complutense University chair and purported malversation linked to university software and use of a Moncloa aide for private work, dropped business corruption counts, returned the passports of Gómez and her assistant Cristina Álvarez, and warned that high-profile spouses can still exert “moral pressure” akin to the precedent set in the Iñaki Urdangarin case.
stories.headline: Madrid court keeps Begoña Gómez case alive but narrows charges and lifts her travel ban

stories.body: A forest fire that began Wednesday in Zaragoza’s Cinco Villas comarca has burned about 12,000 hectares, forced the evacuation of six Aragonese towns plus Petilla de Aragón in neighboring Navarra, cut multiple roads, and required 400 ground crews and 19 aircraft to protect homes overnight in Uncastillo, while simultaneous incidents in Guadalajara and Madrid provinces spurred additional evacuations, UME deployments and even an arson arrest near Lozoyuela.
stories.headline: Wildfire in Zaragoza’s Cinco Villas scorches more than 12,000 hectares

stories.body: From 17 July to 28 August Barcelona’s L1 metro will be closed between Florida and Plaça de Sants so TMB can renew deteriorated track between Santa Eulàlia and Mercat Nou, affecting up to 40,000 daily riders who will rely on replacement buses, alternate metro links or FGC services, while L9/L10 Nord will shut in phases from La Sagrera to Onze de Setembre through 30 August and then to Bon Pastor through 6 September to ready infrastructure for the lines’ central section, with Verdaguer on L4 also closed for accessibility works and the Catalunya L1-L3 transfer corridor sealed for asbestos removal.
stories.headline: Barcelona prepares for major summer shutdowns on Metro lines L1 and L9/L10 for upgrades

stories.body: Ferrocarrils de la Generalitat has rebranded 280 existing information and emergency kiosks across its Barcelona-Vallès and Llobregat-Anoia lines as “purple points,” training operators at the Integrated Command Center to respond round-the-clock to reports of sexual harassment or gender-based violence, adding an anonymous alert function to the FGC mobile app used by 75,000 riders, and launching the “On siguis hi som” campaign—with branded trains—to reassure women they can quickly summon staff support and even private waiting areas if they feel threatened.
stories.headline: FGC turns station help points into 280 ‘purple’ safe spots across the network

stories.body: Barcelona’s city hall chose the joint proposal by Paris-based Bruther and Jorge Vidal Studio to replace the current Sant Jordi Club with a €70 million live-music venue seating 3,000 to 9,000 people by late 2029, doubling capacity, weaving three exterior plazas into Montjuïc’s Olympic Ring, and complementing the Olympic Stadium and Palau Sant Jordi with state-of-the-art production, sustainability and accessibility features across 17,000 square meters and two underground levels.
stories.headline: New Sant Jordi Club overhaul will create a 9,000-seat mid-size arena
